title: Understand: Judicial Branch: Text and History
description: Check your understanding of the the text of Article III and history of the Judicial Branch with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Understand: Judicial Branch: Text and History

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the primary job of the judicial branch?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To make the laws
To enforce the laws
To interpret the laws
To amend the Constitution

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these is a formal constitutional requirement to be appointed as a Supreme Court justice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Must be at least 40 years old
Must have served as a judge in lower court
Must be born in the United States
None of the above

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the phrases that best complete the sentence.**

To become a Supreme Court justice, a person must be nominated by  \_\_\_\_ and confirmed by  \_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the president; the Senate
their home state; the president
the president; the other justices
Congress; direct popular vote

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does Article III say about which courts must exist in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There must be district courts, appellate courts, and a Supreme Court.
There must be a Supreme Court, but Congress can establish lower courts as well.
There can be only a Supreme Court; all other courts are illegal.
There must be lower courts, but a Supreme Court is optional.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Article III of the Constitution says that federal judges will hold their offices for what period of time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: For life, pending good behavior
For two terms of four years each
Until they turn 70 years of age
As long as they keep winning reelection

assessment_items.question_content:
**Federalist No. 78, which focuses on the judicial branch, was authored by which famous founder in 1788?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: John Marshall
John Jay
James Madison
Alexander Hamilton

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did Alexander Hamilton consider the judiciary to be “the least dangerous” branch in the government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The judiciary has no power to declare war.
The judiciary has no influence over how the government spends money.
Judges are weak by nature and easily corruptible.
Judges are easily removed from power by the other branches.

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to Federalist No. 78, why could no legislative act that was contrary to the Constitution ever be valid?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution allows only the president to make the laws.
The Constitution is a higher source of authority than an act of the legislature.
The Supreme Court justices can’t keep track of which laws were unconstitutional.
The people have the authority to rebel against a tyrannical government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In which TWO situations does the Supreme Court have original jurisdiction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Cases involving ambassadors
Cases in which a state is a party
Cases of impeachment
Cases involving disputes between ordinary citizens

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to Federalist No. 78, why is it beneficial for judges to have lifetime appointments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Judges won’t be dependent on public opinion or the support of politicians.
There would be frequent turnover, so the courts’ decisions could change quickly as society shifts.
They would become more powerful than the executive and legislative branches.
Judges’ terms would be short, given the advanced age at which they became judges.

assessment_items.question_content:
Toward the end of Federalist No. 78, Alexander Hamilton directly compares the legislative branch to the judicial branch. 

**What is one difference Hamilton writes about?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Representatives in the legislative branch are governed by the will of the people, but judges are meant to be impartial interpreters of the Constitution.
Representatives aim to undermine and stretch the Constitution, but judges interpret it faithfully.
Representatives seek to gain more power for the legislative branch, but judges seek to keep a balance of power.
Representatives want to reach consensus (unanimous agreement), but judges do not have to reach consensus when they decide cases.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which part of the Constitution gives the federal courts the legal authority to hear and decide a case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Article I, Section 2
Article II, Section 4
Article III, Section 2
Article IV, Section 1
